question: What does 'everlasting gospel' (euangelion aionion) mean in Rev 14:6? Is this the same gospel Paul preached, or a different one? What does the anarthrous construction imply? How does euangelisai connect to the OT basar vocabulary chain (Isa 40:9, 52:7, 61:1)? What is the content of a gospel called 'everlasting'? How does the imperial euangelia thesis relate? Does it pass the Galatians 1:6-9 test?
prior_research_summary: Eight prior studies address related aspects: GSP-12 proved the gospel was planned before creation (Eph 1:4, 2 Tim 1:9, 1 Pet 1:20, Rev 13:8) and traced the aionios semantic range (71 NT occurrences). GSP-10 proved the three angels' messages ARE the gospel (legon construction, Acts 17 parallel, pistis Iesou). GSP-01 proved the gospel is broader than 1 Cor 15:3-4 (22+ NT designations, en protois = 'among first things'). GSP-02 traced the basar-euangelizo LXX bridge (PMI 29.44). 3am-03 established the three-part gospel+judgment+warning pattern across five NT episodes. law-17 analyzed the Galatian heresy (circumcision as salvation requirement, not broader gospel content). Web research (00-web-research.md) provides scholarly debate on the anarthrous construction (Alford vs. Vincent), the imperial euangelia thesis (DeSilva/BBR 2008, Priene inscription), the basar-euangelizo-euangelion triple in Rev 14:6, and the Gal 1:6-9 objection. This study goes deeper on the specific phrase 'everlasting gospel' with fresh tool-driven investigation.

required_verses.reason: Crux text — contains the phrase euangelion aionion, the verb euangelisai, and the content of the first angel's message
Second angel's message — structural component of the everlasting gospel proclamation
Third angel's message — completes the three-part proclamation identified as the everlasting gospel
Descriptive conclusion — commandments of God and faith of Jesus as gospel results
Paul's anathema on 'another gospel' — the Galatians test for whether the everlasting gospel is the same or different
Paul's gospel received by revelation — tests whether the everlasting gospel has the same divine origin
Paul's 'of first importance' gospel summary — often cited as the exclusive definition of the gospel
Paul's second gospel summary — different from 1 Cor 15:1-4, proving the gospel is broader than either summary alone
Gospel as power of God unto salvation — the gospel includes righteousness by faith
Judgment 'according to my gospel' — Paul explicitly includes judgment in gospel content
Direct quotation of Isa 52:7 — bridges OT basar to NT gospel proclamation
Gospel as 'mystery kept secret since the world began' — pre-creation origin of the gospel message
OT basar passage — mevasseret, 'O thou that tellest good tidings to Zion'; herald announcing 'Behold your God!'
OT basar passage — mevasser announcing shalom, tov, yeshuah, malakh; 'Thy God reigneth!'
OT basar passage — levassar, Spirit-anointed herald; quoted by Jesus in Luke 4:18 as his mission statement
Jesus quotes Isa 61:1 and declares 'This day is this scripture fulfilled' — explicit basar-euangelizo bridge
Jesus preaching 'the gospel of the kingdom' — kerysso + legon structure parallels Rev 14:6-7
'This gospel of the kingdom shall be preached in all the world' — universal scope parallel to Rev 14:6
Chosen 'before the foundation of the world' — pre-creation purpose establishing the gospel as eternal
'Before the world began' (pro chronon aionion) — the only NT text using aionion in backward temporal reference
Lamb foreordained 'before the foundation of the world' — pre-creation gospel plan
Lamb slain 'from the foundation of the world' — connects aionion to pre-creation divine purpose
Scripture 'preached before the gospel unto Abraham' — proeuangelizomai, hapax legomenon confirming OT gospel presence
'The gospel preached unto us was preached unto them also' — explicit statement of gospel continuity across testaments
Paul at Athens — contains every element of the first angel's message: Creator God, universal scope, judgment, repentance
The mystery of God 'as he hath declared (euangelise) to his servants the prophets' — links euangelizo to prophetic revelation
OT structural parallel: fear God + keep commandments + judgment — exact triad match with Rev 14:7,12
Paul's gospel confronted by Judaizers demanding circumcision — defines the nature of the 'different gospel'
Circumcision as yoke vs. faith working through love — clarifies what Paul excludes and what he includes
Protoevangelium — the first gospel promise, establishing the gospel as coextensive with human history
Additional Isaiah basar passage — 'first shall say to Zion, Behold, behold them: and I will give to Jerusalem one that bringeth good tidings'
Additional Isaiah basar passage — 'they shall shew forth the praises of the LORD'
Non-salvific use of basar — Ahimaaz brings tidings of military victory; Expositor's Greek Testament cites this for euangelisai in Rev 14:6
Angelic euangelizo — 'I bring you good tidings of great joy'; angel using euangelizo parallels Rev 14:6
Peter: 'preaching peace by Jesus Christ' — echoes Isa 52:7's shalom announcement
Mystery of his will + gospel of salvation — gospel encompasses God's cosmic purpose
'Unsearchable riches of Christ' + eternal purpose — gospel breadth exceeds any single summary
Gospel of peace — one of 22+ NT designations showing gospel multidimensionality
Gospel preached 'in all the world' + mystery 'hid from ages' — universal scope and eternal hiddenness
'Them that obey not the gospel' — gospel demands obedience, not merely intellectual assent
'Glorious gospel of the blessed God' — another NT designation
'Eternal life, which God, that cannot lie, promised before the world began' — aionion in temporal backward reference
Harvest scene immediately following the three angels — the proclamation leads to the Son of Man's coming
Sabbath commandment echoing creation language — Rev 14:7's 'made heaven, earth, sea' parallels Exod 20:11 exactly
Paul's pre-conversion zeal — context for understanding what the Galatian heresy involved
Jerusalem Council — 'Except ye be circumcised after the manner of Moses, ye cannot be saved'
'Circumcision is nothing' — Paul explicitly nullifies circumcision while affirming commandment-keeping
John's judgment preaching labeled euangelizo — judgment IS gospel
'Glorious gospel of Christ' — gospel vocabulary diversity
'Striving together for the faith of the gospel' — pistis + euangelion connection
'Gospel of God' + 'word of God' — gospel as divine word, not human invention
'Faith once delivered unto the saints' — the gospel as a fixed deposit, not an evolving message

focus_areas.title: The anarthrous construction of euangelion aionion in Rev 14:6
focus_areas.what: Determine whether the absence of the article before euangelion implies indefiniteness ('an eternal gospel'), qualitativeness ('eternal-gospel'), or definiteness despite anarthrous form ('the eternal gospel')
focus_areas.why: Scholars divide sharply: Alford argues euangelion is a technical term that remains definite without the article (citing Rom 1:1 as parallel); Vincent emphasizes the qualitative/universal character; DeSilva proposes 'an age-long accession announcement.' The answer determines whether this is THE gospel or a distinct category.
focus_areas.how: Check all 77 NT occurrences of euangelion (G2098) for article presence/absence patterns. READ the library entry $WORD_STUDIES/G2098-euangelion.md for occurrence data. Run greek_parser.py on Rev 14:6 and Rom 1:1 to compare constructions. READ the grammar reference $GRAMMAR_REF/greek/anarthrous-predicate-nominative.md for rules on anarthrous nouns. Check whether euangelion is ever used in a non-technical sense in the NT.
focus_areas.narrative: This is the decisive linguistic question for distinguishing interpretive camps. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G2098-euangelion.md` — note the full occurrence count (77x in 74 verses) and how often euangelion appears with vs. without the article. The Alford position holds that euangelion had become 'so technical' a term that no argument can be derived from the missing article. Verify this by checking every anarthrous euangelion in the NT: Rom 1:1, 2 Cor 4:3-4, Gal 1:6-7, Phil 1:27, etc. Run `python greek_parser.py --verse 'REV 14:6'` and `python greek_parser.py --verse 'ROM 1:1'` to compare morphology. READ `$GRAMMAR_REF/greek/anarthrous-predicate-nominative.md` — though Rev 14:6 is not a predicate nominative construction, the principles about qualitative vs. indefinite vs. definite anarthrous nouns apply. The adjective aionion (G166) also lacks the article, making both noun and modifier anarthrous. Does this double-anarthrous pattern occur elsewhere with euangelion? Run `python cross_testament_parallels_v2.py --hybrid-nt 'REV 14:6'` to find structural parallels.

focus_areas.title: The basar-euangelizo-euangelion vocabulary chain from OT to Rev 14:6
focus_areas.what: Trace the Hebrew basar (H1319) through LXX euangelizo (G2097) to the unique noun+verb combination in Rev 14:6
focus_areas.why: Rev 14:6 is reportedly the only NT verse where euangelion (noun) and euangelisai (verb) appear together. This makes the verse a deliberate vocabulary convergence point, tying the angel's proclamation to the OT herald tradition in Isaiah.
focus_areas.how: READ $WORD_STUDIES/H1319-basar.md and $WORD_STUDIES/G2097-euangelizo.md for full occurrence data. Run search_strongs.py --lxx-map H1319 to verify the basar→euangelizo LXX bridge. Run hebrew_parser.py on Isa 40:9, 52:7, 61:1 for morphological analysis of basar in each passage. Check NT quotations of these Isaiah passages (Rom 10:15 quoting Isa 52:7; Luke 4:18 quoting Isa 61:1). READ $WORD_STUDIES/TR-basar-euangelizo.md for the translation relationship study.
focus_areas.narrative: The vocabulary chain is the strongest evidence for gospel continuity across testaments. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/H1319-basar.md` — note the Piel stem in 96% of occurrences and the 5 Isaiah usages (40:9, 41:27, 52:7, 60:6, 61:1). READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G2097-euangelizo.md` for the NT verb data. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/TR-basar-euangelizo.md` for the LXX bridge (PMI 29.44). Run `python hebrew_parser.py --verse 'Isa 40:9'`, `python hebrew_parser.py --verse 'Isa 52:7'`, and `python hebrew_parser.py --verse 'Isa 61:1'` to examine the Piel participle mevasser/mevasseret forms. The three Isaiah passages establish a cumulative herald theology: Isa 40:9 announces 'Behold your God!', Isa 52:7 announces shalom+tov+yeshuah+malakh ('Thy God reigneth!'), and Isa 61:1 announces Spirit-anointed deliverance. Paul explicitly bridges these in Rom 10:15 (quoting Isa 52:7) and Luke records Jesus bridging them in Luke 4:18 (quoting Isa 61:1). The angel in Rev 14:6 uses BOTH the noun and verb from the same root — euangelion + euangelisai — creating a deliberate literary echo of the Isaianic herald. Verify the claim that this is the only NT co-occurrence of noun and verb by searching for G2097 and G2098 co-occurrences in the same verse. Also check 2 Sam 18:19-20 for the non-salvific use of basar that the Expositor's Greek Testament cites.

focus_areas.title: The semantic range of aionios (G166) — 'everlasting,' 'eternal,' or 'age-long'?
focus_areas.what: Determine whether aionios in Rev 14:6 means 'eternal/everlasting' (without beginning or end), 'age-long' (spanning the ages, DeSilva's proposal), or something else
focus_areas.why: DeSilva proposes translating euangelion aionion as 'an age-long accession announcement,' reducing aionios from 'eternal' to 'spanning an age.' If correct, this changes the theological claim from 'a gospel that is eternal' to 'a gospel announced at a key historical moment.' The distinction affects whether the gospel is identified as transcending time or merely as a timely announcement.
focus_areas.how: READ $WORD_STUDIES/G166-aionios.md for the full 71-occurrence dataset. Check 2 Tim 1:9 specifically — pro chronon aionion uses aionios in backward temporal reference, confirming it can reach backward in time (before creation). Check aionios in contexts where duration vs. quality is at issue: Matt 25:46 (eternal punishment/eternal life), John 3:16, John 17:3, Rom 6:23. READ $WORD_STUDIES/G165-aion.md for the root noun. READ $WORD_STUDIES/H5769-owlam.md and $WORD_STUDIES/TR-owlam-aion.md for the Hebrew-Greek bridge.
focus_areas.narrative: This focus area settles whether the gospel in Rev 14:6 is being identified by its temporal scope or its essential nature. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G166-aionios.md` — note the 71 occurrences and the distribution of meanings. The critical test case is 2 Tim 1:9, where pro chronon aionion means 'before eternal times' or 'before ages of time' — here aionios reaches backward before creation. If aionios can refer to pre-creation time, it cannot be reduced to 'age-long' in the sense of a single historical period. Also check Matt 25:46, where aionios modifies BOTH punishment and life — if 'age-long' means temporary for punishment, it means temporary for life too, which no interpreter accepts. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G165-aion.md` for the noun aion. Run `python search_strongs.py --lookup G166` to verify occurrence count. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/H5769-owlam.md` for the Hebrew equivalent and `$WORD_STUDIES/TR-owlam-aion.md` for the LXX bridge. The Hebrew owlam (199 OT uses in 'everlasting/forever' contexts) is consistently rendered aionios in the LXX, establishing a semantic tradition that Rev 14:6 inherits.

focus_areas.title: The content of the everlasting gospel — what does the angel actually say?
focus_areas.what: Analyze the specific content of Rev 14:7 as the stated content of the euangelion aionion: fear God, give glory, judgment hour, worship the Creator
focus_areas.why: Critics (Answering Adventism, Cambridge Bible) argue either that this content is 'pure natural theism' (not the Pauline kerygma) or that any content beyond 1 Cor 15:3-4 corrupts the gospel. The actual text of Rev 14:7 must be examined to determine whether its content is (a) consistent with or (b) alien to the broader NT gospel vocabulary.
focus_areas.how: READ $GRAMMAR_REF/passages/rev-14-6-12.md for the full grammar analysis. Parse Rev 14:7 word-by-word: phobethete (fear — aorist passive imperative), dote doxan (give glory), elthen he hora tes kriseos (the hour of judgment came), proskynhsate to poiesanti (worship the one who made). Compare each element to Paul's preaching: Acts 17:22-31 (Creator, judgment, repentance), Rom 2:16 (judgment according to my gospel), 2 Thess 1:8 (gospel obedience). Compare to Eccl 12:13-14 (fear God + commandments + judgment = exact triad match).
focus_areas.narrative: The legon construction in Rev 14:7 (present active participle of lego, G3004) introduces the direct discourse of the euangelisai from v.6. What follows legon IS the content of the everlasting gospel. READ `$GRAMMAR_REF/passages/rev-14-6-12.md` — note the aorist imperative cluster (phobethete, dote, proskynhsate) and the causal hoti clause introducing the judgment hour. READ `$GRAMMAR_REF/greek/participle-legon-discourse.md` for the recitative function. The research agent should run `python greek_parser.py --verse 'REV 14:7'` and compare the imperatives with the Pauline vocabulary in Acts 17:24-31. The element-by-element match established in GSP-10 needs verification: Creator God (Acts 17:24 = Rev 14:7), universal scope (Acts 17:26,30 = Rev 14:6), judgment (Acts 17:31 = Rev 14:7), repentance (Acts 17:30 = Rev 14:7). If every element of Rev 14:7 appears in Paul's preaching at Athens, the 'different gospel' objection collapses. Also compare with Eccl 12:13-14, which provides an exact OT triad: fear God + keep commandments + judgment — the three elements of the first angel's message. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G5399-phobeo.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G1391-doxa.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G2920-krisis.md`, `$WORD_STUDIES/G4352-proskyneo.md` for the individual vocabulary items.

focus_areas.title: The Galatians 1:6-9 test — does the everlasting gospel pass?
focus_areas.what: Determine what Paul's anathema on 'another gospel' actually targets: additions to gospel content, or perversions of the gospel principle of grace
focus_areas.why: The 'Answering Adventism' objection argues that Paul's anathema means the three angels' messages must contain nothing beyond 1 Cor 15:1-4. If adding judgment, Sabbath, or commandment-keeping constitutes preaching 'another gospel,' then Rev 14:6-12 would be cursed by Paul's own standard. This is the central polemic question.
focus_areas.how: Parse Gal 1:6-9 for the heteros/allos distinction: heteron (G2087, different in kind) vs. allo (G243, another of the same kind). READ $WORD_STUDIES/G2087-heteros.md and $WORD_STUDIES/G243-allos.md. Identify the specific heresy Paul condemns: circumcision as salvation requirement (Gal 2:3-4, 5:2-3, 6:12-13, Acts 15:1). Does Paul condemn adding eschatological content or adding works-righteousness conditions? Check Paul's OWN gospel for elements beyond death/burial/resurrection: Rom 1:1-4, 2:16, 16:25-26, Eph 3:8-11.
focus_areas.narrative: This focus area must be handled with surgical precision. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G2087-heteros.md` and `$WORD_STUDIES/G243-allos.md` — the heteros/allos distinction in Gal 1:6-7 is NOT a simple synonym pair. Paul says they are turning to heteron euangelion (a gospel different in KIND), then immediately adds ho ouk estin allo ('which is not another of the same kind'). The implication: what the Judaizers preach is so different it does not deserve the name 'gospel' at all. Run `python greek_parser.py --verse 'GAL 1:6'`, `python greek_parser.py --verse 'GAL 1:7'`, `python greek_parser.py --verse 'GAL 1:8'`. The research agent should retrieve the FULL text of Gal 1-2 with chapter context to identify what the Galatian heresy actually was. Gal 2:3-4 names circumcision. Gal 5:2-3: 'if ye be circumcised, Christ shall profit you nothing.' Gal 6:12-13: the troublemakers want to 'glory in your flesh.' The heresy is making circumcision (a works-righteousness condition) necessary for salvation — NOT adding eschatological information. Paul himself includes judgment in his gospel (Rom 2:16), the law in his preaching (Rom 3:31; Gal 5:14), and creation in his theology (Rom 1:20; Acts 17:24). If these inclusions don't violate Gal 1:6-9, neither does Rev 14:7's content. Also check 1 Cor 7:19 — 'circumcision is nothing... but the keeping of the commandments of God' — Paul nullifies circumcision while AFFIRMING commandment-keeping, exactly the position of Rev 14:12.

focus_areas.title: The imperial euangelia thesis — Rev 14:6 as counter-proclamation to Caesar
focus_areas.what: Evaluate whether Rev 14:6 functions as a deliberate counter-proclamation to Roman imperial announcements, and whether this background illuminates or replaces the theological meaning
focus_areas.why: DeSilva (BBR 2008) proposes that euangelion aionion parallels Roman imperial accession proclamations. The Priene inscription (9 BC) called Augustus's birthday 'the beginning of the good tidings for the world.' If John uses imperial vocabulary deliberately, this adds a political dimension to the everlasting gospel. But does the imperial background replace the theological content, or does John subvert the imperial vocabulary to proclaim a higher truth?
focus_areas.how: Search for imperial cult vocabulary elsewhere in Revelation to determine if John sustains a counter-imperial pattern: kyrios (Rev 17:14, 19:16), soter, basileus (Rev 19:16), and proskyneo (worship language throughout Rev 13-14). If John consistently subverts imperial titles, the euangelion usage is part of a larger pattern, not an isolated borrowing. Check whether Mark 1:1 ('beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God') parallels the Priene inscription's 'beginning of the good tidings for the world.'
focus_areas.narrative: The imperial euangelia thesis is a legitimate historical-contextual observation that must be evaluated against the text. DeSilva proposes translating euangelion aionion as 'an age-long accession announcement,' paralleling Vespasian's accession sequence. But DeSilva himself acknowledges that 'many are clear to distinguish this gospel from the Gospel.' The research agent should search for imperial cult vocabulary throughout Revelation: Rev 13 (beast demanding worship), Rev 17:14 and 19:16 ('King of kings, Lord of lords' — imperial title usurpation), Rev 1:5 ('ruler of the kings of the earth'). If John consistently uses imperial vocabulary to subvert it, then euangelion in Rev 14:6 is not a neutral borrowing but a deliberate counter-claim: the true euangelion belongs to God, not Caesar. Run `python cross_testament_parallels_v2.py --hybrid-nt 'REV 14:6'` to find NT parallels. The Priene inscription is historical background, not biblical evidence, but it illuminates WHY John's audience would hear euangelion as a politically charged term. Mark 1:1 provides the NT precedent: 'The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, Son of God' directly echoes and subverts the Augustan formula. Run `python greek_parser.py --verse 'MRK 1:1'` to compare vocabulary.

focus_areas.title: The pre-creation origin of the gospel — what does 'everlasting' reach back to?
focus_areas.what: Determine whether aionion in Rev 14:6 includes backward temporal reference (before creation) or only forward (lasting forever into the future)
focus_areas.why: If the gospel is 'everlasting' only in the forward direction, it could be a NT-era message with permanent effects. If it reaches backward before creation, the gospel was always God's plan, and every OT expression of it was the same gospel anticipated. This distinction determines whether the everlasting gospel is a message ABOUT eternal things or a message that IS itself eternal.
focus_areas.how: Examine four independent pre-creation texts: Eph 1:4, 2 Tim 1:9, 1 Pet 1:18-20, Rev 13:8. READ $WORD_STUDIES/G166-aionios.md for how aionios handles backward vs. forward temporality. Check Gal 3:8 (proeuangelizomai — 'gospeled-beforehand' to Abraham) and Heb 4:2 ('the gospel preached unto us was preached unto them also'). These texts together establish that the gospel is not a NT invention but a pre-creation divine purpose progressively revealed.
focus_areas.narrative: GSP-12 established this in detail, but this study needs to go deeper on the specific linguistic evidence. The key text is 2 Tim 1:9: πρὸ χρόνων αἰωνίων (pro chronon aionion) — 'before times eternal/age-long.' Here aionios explicitly reaches BACKWARD before creation. If aionios can describe pre-temporal reality in 2 Tim 1:9, it does the same in Rev 14:6: the gospel is 'everlasting' in both directions. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G166-aionios.md` — check the occurrence in 2 Tim 1:9 specifically. Also check Tit 1:2-3: 'eternal life, which God promised before the world began (pro chronon aionion).' The same phrase appears in both 2 Tim 1:9 and Tit 1:2, establishing a Pauline pattern. Eph 1:4 ('chosen in him before the foundation of the world'), 1 Pet 1:20 ('foreordained before the foundation of the world'), and Rev 13:8 ('Lamb slain from the foundation of the world') provide independent witnesses. Run `python greek_parser.py --verse '2TI 1:9'` and `python greek_parser.py --verse 'TIT 1:2'`. Gal 3:8 uses the hapax proeuangelizomai (G4283) — 'gospeled-beforehand' — a word Paul COINED to express that the gospel existed before the NT era. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G4283-proeuangelizomai.md`.

focus_areas.title: Rev 14:12 as gospel result — commandments + faith of Jesus
focus_areas.what: Determine whether Rev 14:12 describes the RESULT of accepting the everlasting gospel or an ADDITION to it
focus_areas.why: Critics argue that commandment-keeping and faith in Rev 14:12 are 'additions' to the gospel (cf. Gal 1:6-9 objection). If Rev 14:12 is a prescription (things you must DO to be saved), it may conflict with grace. If it is a description (what people who accept the gospel LOOK LIKE), it is consistent with Paul's 'faith working through love' (Gal 5:6).
focus_areas.how: Parse Rev 14:12 for the participial construction: hoi terountes (present active participle of tereo, G5083) is DESCRIPTIVE, not IMPERATIVE. The saints are identified BY their keeping, not commanded to keep. Compare with Gal 5:6 ('faith which worketh by love'), Gal 5:22-23 (fruit of the Spirit), Rom 8:4 ('righteousness of the law fulfilled in us'). READ $WORD_STUDIES/G5083-tereo.md, $WORD_STUDIES/G4102-pistis.md, $WORD_STUDIES/G1785-entole.md.
focus_areas.narrative: This is where the Galatians 1:6-9 objection meets the text of Revelation. READ `$GRAMMAR_REF/passages/rev-14-6-12.md` — note that hoi terountes (the ones keeping) is an articular present active participle functioning as a substantival descriptor, not an imperative. It identifies the saints, it does not command them. GSP-10 established that terountes is descriptive, not prescriptive. This study should verify that reading by checking the Greek syntax. Run `python greek_parser.py --verse 'REV 14:12'`. The coordination of tas entolas tou Theou (commandments of God) with ten pistin Iesou (faith of Jesus) under ONE participle means these are not separate identities but a single composite description. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G5083-tereo.md` for tereo's usage pattern, `$WORD_STUDIES/G4102-pistis.md` for pistis Iesou (subjective vs. objective genitive), `$WORD_STUDIES/G1785-entole.md` for entole. Compare Gal 5:6: pistis di agapes energoumene ('faith working through love') — Paul's own formulation combines faith and works-producing love. Compare 1 Cor 7:19: 'circumcision is nothing... but the keeping of the commandments of God' — Paul explicitly values commandment-keeping while rejecting circumcision. Rev 14:12 is not an addition to the gospel but a description of what the gospel produces in those who receive it.

focus_areas.title: The uniqueness of Rev 14:6 — euangelion + euangelisai together
focus_areas.what: Verify and analyze the claim that Rev 14:6 is the only NT verse where the noun euangelion (G2098) and the verb euangelizo (G2097) appear together in the same clause
focus_areas.why: If this co-occurrence is unique, it represents a deliberate literary and theological intensification — the angel does not merely 'preach' or merely 'have a message' but has 'a gospel to gospel.' This redundancy makes Rev 14:6 the most concentrated gospel-vocabulary verse in the NT.
focus_areas.how: Search for all NT co-occurrences of G2097 and G2098 in the same verse using search_strongs.py. Verify the uniqueness claim. If confirmed, analyze the rhetorical effect: the cognate accusative construction (echonta euangelion... euangelisai) functions like 'dreaming a dream' or 'seeing a vision' — it intensifies the action by naming both the object and the act from the same root.
focus_areas.narrative: The web research (BiblePortal, G2097 study) claims Rev 14:6 is the only NT verse where noun and verb from the euangel- root appear together. This needs tool verification. Run `python search_strongs.py --verses G2097` and `python search_strongs.py --verses G2098` to get the full verse lists, then cross-reference for any overlap. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G2098-euangelion.md` and `$WORD_STUDIES/G2097-euangelizo.md` for the complete occurrence data. If the co-occurrence is confirmed as unique, this is a remarkable literary feature: echonta euangelion aionion euangelisai literally says 'having an everlasting gospel to evangelize' — a cognate construction where the object of the infinitive shares the same root as the infinitive itself. This figure of speech (cognate accusative or figura etymologica) intensifies meaning in both Hebrew (e.g., 'dying you shall die,' Gen 2:17) and Greek. The effect in Rev 14:6 is to make the gospel-ness of the proclamation unmistakable. Also note that Rev 14:6 is the LAST occurrence of euangelion in the Bible — the word 'gospel' never appears again after this verse. Run `python search_strongs.py --verses G2098` and verify whether any later verse uses the noun.

focus_areas.title: The Rev 14:7 / Exod 20:11 creation language parallel
focus_areas.what: Analyze whether Rev 14:7's 'worship him that made heaven, and the earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters' deliberately echoes Exodus 20:11's Sabbath commandment language
focus_areas.why: If the first angel's creation language is drawn from the fourth commandment, the call to 'worship the Creator' becomes a specific call to honor the Sabbath commandment rather than a generic appeal to theism. This would refute the Cambridge Bible's 'pure natural theism' interpretation and connect the everlasting gospel to a specific commandment.
focus_areas.how: Compare the fourfold creation catalog in Rev 14:7 (heaven, earth, sea, fountains) with Exod 20:11 (heaven, earth, sea, and all that in them is). Run hebrew_parser.py on Exod 20:11 and greek_parser.py on Rev 14:7 to compare vocabulary. Check whether John's creation language appears elsewhere in Revelation (Rev 4:11, 10:6). READ $WORD_STUDIES/G4160-poieo.md for the verb 'made.'
focus_areas.narrative: Rev 14:7 uses a fourfold creation catalog: ton ouranon kai ten gen kai thalassan kai pegas hydaton ('heaven and earth and sea and fountains of waters'). Exod 20:11 has a parallel structure: 'the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them is.' The overlap is not exact — Rev 14:7 has 'fountains of waters' where Exod 20:11 has 'all that in them is' — but the structural parallel is striking. Check Rev 10:6 for another creation formula in Revelation: 'him that liveth for ever and ever, who created heaven, and the things that therein are, and the earth, and the things that therein are, and the sea, and the things which are therein.' Run `python greek_parser.py --verse 'REV 14:7'`, `python greek_parser.py --verse 'REV 10:6'`, and `python greek_parser.py --verse 'REV 4:11'`. Run `python hebrew_parser.py --verse 'Exo 20:11'`. If the creation language in Rev 14:7 echoes the Sabbath commandment, then the 'everlasting gospel' includes a call to Sabbath worship as part of its Creator-worship imperative. This connects to Rev 14:12's 'commandments of God' — the saints who respond to the first angel's message are those who keep God's commandments, including the one that grounds worship in creation.

focus_areas.title: Paul's own gospel as broader than 1 Cor 15:1-4
focus_areas.what: Demonstrate from Paul's own writings that 'the gospel' includes more than death, burial, and resurrection
focus_areas.why: The critical objection (Answering Adventism) assumes 1 Cor 15:1-4 is the complete and exclusive definition of the gospel. If Paul himself gives different gospel summaries in different letters, this assumption is falsified — both summaries are partial descriptions of a richer reality.
focus_areas.how: Compare Paul's gospel summaries: 1 Cor 15:1-4 (death/burial/resurrection), Rom 1:1-4 (descendant of David/declared Son of God), Rom 2:16 (judgment according to my gospel), Rom 16:25-26 (mystery kept secret/now revealed), Eph 3:8-11 (unsearchable riches/eternal purpose). Parse en protois in 1 Cor 15:3 — does it mean 'as the only things' or 'among the first things'? READ $WORD_STUDIES/G2098-euangelion.md for the 22+ NT designations of the gospel.
focus_areas.narrative: GSP-01 established this argument in detail, but the research agent should verify the key texts independently. The critical move is 1 Cor 15:3: Paul says he delivered en protois (ἐν πρώτοις) — 'among the first things' or 'of first importance.' Run `python greek_parser.py --verse '1CO 15:3'` to parse en protois. If en protois means 'of first importance,' then 1 Cor 15:3-4 is a priority statement, not an exclusivity statement. Paul is saying 'these are the MOST important things,' not 'these are the ONLY things.' Verify this by checking Rom 1:1-4, where Paul gives a completely different gospel summary (descended from David, declared Son of God with power by the resurrection). If 1 Cor 15:1-4 were the COMPLETE definition, Paul would be preaching a 'different gospel' in Romans — which fails his own Gal 1:6-9 test. Also check Paul's 22+ NT designations for the gospel: gospel of the kingdom (Matt 4:23), gospel of God (Rom 1:1), gospel of Christ (Rom 1:16), gospel of peace (Eph 6:15), gospel of salvation (Eph 1:13), glorious gospel (2 Cor 4:4), etc. Each designation highlights a different facet. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G2098-euangelion.md` for the full list. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/WG-gospel.md` for the word group.

word_studies.description: euangelion (gospel) — 77 NT occurrences, article patterns, 22+ designations
euangelizo (to preach gospel) — 55 NT occurrences, LXX bridge to basar
basar (to bring good tidings) — Hebrew verb, 24 OT occurrences, Piel stem
allos (another of same kind) — Gal 1:7 contrast with heteros
krisis (judgment) — 47 NT occurrences, OT riyb background
proskyneo (to worship) — worship vocabulary in Revelation
tereo (to keep) — Rev 14:12 'those keeping the commandments'
mysterion (mystery) — Rev 10:7 'mystery of God'; Eph 3:4 'mystery of Christ'
mesouranema (mid-heaven) — Rev 14:6 angel flying in mid-heaven
poieo (to make/do) — Rev 14:7 'the one who made'
krino (to judge) — verbal form of judgment
yare (to fear) — Hebrew background for phobeo
kosmos (world) — 'before the foundation of the world' texts
lego (to say) — legon participle in Rev 14:7
Translation relationship: riyb → krisis, covenant lawsuit background
Translation relationship: yare → phobeo, fear/reverence bridge
Translation relationship: shachah → proskyneo, worship bridge
Translation relationship: kabad → doxazo, glory bridge
Translation relationship: kabod → doxa, glory noun bridge
Translation relationship: shaphat → krino, judgment bridge
Word group: gospel family — euangelion, euangelizo, euangelistes, proeuangelizomai

research_instructions:
This study is Study 4 of the 3AM series and begins Arc 1: The First Angel. Cross-references to prior GSP and 3AM studies are permitted but must supplement original investigation. The study must go DEEPER than prior studies on the specific phrase 'everlasting gospel' and its implications.

**Critical investigations:**
1. The anarthrous construction debate requires checking EVERY NT occurrence of euangelion (G2098) for article presence/absence — this has not been done in prior studies.
2. The imperial euangelia thesis (DeSilva/Priene inscription) needs evaluation of whether John uses imperial vocabulary elsewhere in Revelation as a sustained counter-proclamation pattern.
3. The verb+noun co-occurrence in Rev 14:6 (euangelisai + euangelion) — verify this is unique in the NT.
4. The Rev 14:7 / Exod 20:11 creation language parallel needs side-by-side lexical comparison.
5. The Gal 1:6-9 test must distinguish between Paul condemning additions to gospel CONTENT vs. perversions of gospel PRINCIPLE (substituting works-righteousness for grace).

**Connection to Rev 10:7:** Meyer links euangelisai in Rev 14:6 to euangelise in Rev 10:7. Check this connection — does the 'mystery of God' in Rev 10:7 relate to the 'everlasting gospel' in Rev 14:6?

**Rev 14:6 as the last euangelion:** Verify whether Rev 14:6 is the final occurrence of euangelion (G2098) in the Bible. If so, this is the last time Scripture uses the word 'gospel' — its placement in the final prophetic proclamation is theologically significant.
